description:
You are developing a tiny logging helper for another engineer who needs to capture the IPv4 addresses of a few well-known hostnames.  
Complete the following mini-task entirely from the command line, using only standard GNU/Linux user-level tools (no sudo).

Goal  
1. Create the directory /home/user/network_reports if it does not already exist.  
2. Inside that directory create a new file named dns_snapshot.log (overwrite any existing file with the same name).  
3. Resolve the hostnames below and write the results to dns_snapshot.log in the exact order shown:  
   • example.com  
   • localhost  
4. The log file must follow the **exact** format below so that automated tests can validate it:

   a. One line per hostname.  
   b. Each line contains:  
      hostname, one single space, the IPv4 address, and then **nothing else** on that line (no trailing spaces).  
   c. After the last hostname line, insert **one** completely blank line.  
   d. Immediately after that blank line, add the summary line:  
      Total entries: 2  

   The finished file must look *exactly* like this (except for the final newline character at the end of the file, which is required):

   example.com 93.184.216.34  
   localhost 127.0.0.1  

   Total entries: 2  

5. When everything is done, you should be able to display the file with “cat /home/user/network_reports/dns_snapshot.log” and see precisely the four lines shown above (including the single blank line).  

Hints  
• Any common resolver tool (getent, dig, or nslookup) is acceptable.  
• Redirect or echo the output so that no extra characters (tabs, multiple spaces, comments) appear.  
• Remember to ensure the directory exists before creating the file.
truth:
Expected artefacts created by the agent:

1. Directory  
   /home/user/network_reports   (must exist, normal user permissions 755)

2. Log file  
   /home/user/network_reports/dns_snapshot.log   (permissions 644 or more open)

3. Exact required file content (four lines including the blank line, each terminated by a single ASCII newline “\n”):

line 1: "example.com 93.184.216.34\n"  
line 2: "localhost 127.0.0.1\n"  
line 3: "\n"    (blank line containing only the newline character)  
line 4: "Total entries: 2\n"  

No leading BOM, no trailing spaces, no extra blank lines.
name: task_000000_826247cf
